title: How we made Atelier upgrade itself
lead: Self-hosted Drupal upgrades are scary and manual — which is exactly why most installs go stale. Here's how we made the site heal itself on a new image.
author: Moana Lucy
author_bio: Platform engineer at Atelier. Writes about Drupal, containers, and making hard things boring.
body_md:
The single biggest reason self-hosted CMS installs don't reach a million happy users isn't features — it's that **upgrades are frightening**. So for Atelier we made one rule: pulling a new image and restarting should be the entire upgrade.

## The split that makes it work

We separate *immutable code* (baked into the image) from *persistent state* (the database and files, on volumes). Because the code is frozen in the image, the only way new code arrives is a new image — which means upgrades are atomic and reversible at the code layer.

> Restarting on a new image *is* the upgrade. There is no separate migrate step to remember.

## Converge, don't migrate

On every start, a converge routine makes the database match the code in the image:

- Empty database → fresh install from the recipe.
- Existing database → snapshot, run `drush updatedb`, rebuild caches, health-check, and roll back to the snapshot if anything looks wrong.

Run it on a healthy site and it's a no-op. Run it on a half-broken one and it re-runs whatever was missed. That's the whole trick — **idempotent convergence** instead of fragile, hand-ordered migration steps.

### What we'd tell our past selves

Don't ship a config snapshot and call it a distribution. Ship the code, and let the running site converge to it. The database is the user's; the image is yours.
